data.title: Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

U.S. small-cap stocks ended the quarter with sizable gains, but the march forward was volatile. Initially, stocks turned lower after President Trump unveiled tariff rates for countries that were much higher than investors had anticipated. However, equities then rebounded sharply, as the President’s economic team appeared to be softening its stance on tariffs. For the quarter, the Russell 2500™ Index was up 8.59%, while the Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund—Investor Class gained 0.45%.

Much of the Fund’s underperformance relative to the benchmark was due to our long holdings, which collectively did not keep pace with the Index. As investor views on tariffs shifted, a “risk-on” environment followed for much of the quarter. Against this backdrop, the stocks of lower-quality companies and those with more speculative business models generally outperformed those of higher-quality businesses. This was a headwind for the Fund’s long portfolio, given that our investment style focuses on finding high-quality companies. And that headwind was severe. By some metrics this was one of the strongest environments favoring low-quality stocks that we’ve experienced.

For perspective, one way we gauge whether high-quality or low-quality stocks are in favor is through a proprietary process, in which we screen and rank the small-cap universe by several aggregate factors, including financial metrics such as return on assets and operating profit margins, among other items. We then compare how the companies in the top quintile of this aggregate score (high-quality businesses) perform against the bottom quintile (low-quality businesses). Based on that metric, this was the third-strongest 10-week period of low-quality outperformance of the last decade, and the strongest since early 2021. Given that headwind, we weren’t surprised to see our long portfolio underperform.

Details of the quarter

Among our long positions, the largest detractor from Fund performance was Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY). The company is one of the largest homebuilders in North America, specializing in modular homes for the single-family, multi-family and hospitality segments. Champion’s stock was down after company management reported weaker than expected quarterly operating results. High interest rates make home payments more expensive, so some of Champion’s customers are offsetting that expense by opting out of some of the higher-grade amenities within modular houses. This is a headwind for Champion’s earnings because those amenities tend to improve margins for the company. The short-term results haven’t changed our view of the company. In an environment where homebuilders face significant challenges, we were pleased to see Champion increase revenue 10.7% in its most recent quarter over the same period a year ago.

Another detractor was National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), a real-estate investment trust that owns and operates self-storage facilities. The outlook of higher-for-longer interest rates likely weighed on the stock. For National Storage, higher rates reduce home sales, which in turn reduces the need for storage space. However, we remain confident in the company’s long-term outlook and maintained our position. We like National Storage for several reasons: Its market is large and growing. The company also has opportunities to acquire smaller competitors. Additionally, we’re impressed with National Storage’s management team, which we’ve known for many years.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc. (INSP) also detracted. The company develops minimally invasive solutions for patients with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). The second quarter saw the long-awaited launch of Inspire V, the company’s new device that greatly simplifies the medical installation procedure. The stock likely sold off because medical professionals will need to work through their inventory of Inspire IV prior to adopting the new product. Inspire V extends the company’s lead in the OSA space and should result in higher volume across its install base in the second half the year.

One of the largest contributors on the long side was Fabrinet (FN). Fabrinet makes optical components that are used in data centers. The business benefits from the increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI). While fundamentals for the company remain strong, AI has been a popular investment theme and that likely played a larger role in fueling the stock’s rise.

Another contributor was Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE), an enterprise software provider for the property and casualty insurance industry. Guidewire has continued to benefit as insurance companies have increasingly replaced their core (usually on-premises) legacy software with centralized, cloud-based offerings. We’ve been impressed with how Guidewire’s technology suite is gaining traction among insurers.

HealthEquity, Inc. (HQY) also contributed. The company is the largest U.S. non-bank custodian for health savings accounts (HSAs). HealthEquity’s stock rebounded after having lost ground in late March and April. At that time, management said it faced higher service costs as it sought to prevent fraud and cybersecurity threats. Like other financial firms, HealthEquity has experienced an increase in such threats. But after speaking with management, we felt the steps they’ve been taking to address fraud and cyber threats are sensible. We’ve also been pleased to see the company prioritize various security initiatives that were already under way to help mitigate the problem. Additionally, we’ve been encouraged by the strength of HealthEquity’s underlying business trends.

Turning to short positions, detractors from Fund performance are those stocks that rose in price. One of the largest detractors was Rocket Lab Corp. (RKLB), a space company that designs, manufactures and launches rockets and spacecraft. Investors have been enthusiastic about companies tied to space exploration, in part due to SpaceX’s innovation around reusable rockets. However, we don’t see any competitive advantages for Rocket Lab and believe the company’s path to growth is far from certain.

Among shorts, contributors to Fund performance are those stocks that declined in price. One of the largest contributors was Sweetgreen, Inc. (SG), a fast-casual restaurant chain that specializes in made-to-order salads and grain bowls. We short the position as a hedge against some of our long positions in the consumer discretionary and consumer staples sectors. Such companies can be impacted when consumer spending turns weak. This quarter, the stock was likely down due to fears that a potential trade war could lead to inflation and a tougher spending environment. (Current and future holdings are subject to change and risk.)

Outlook and positioning

While the Fund underperformed its benchmark, we remain constructive about the positioning of our long holdings. As we mentioned earlier, in the second quarter’s strong risk-on environment, lower quality stocks outperformed. Typically, high-quality stocks trade at valuations higher than low-quality stocks, but the gap in valuations between the two has narrowed. We believe that will translate into higher expected returns for our brand of high-quality investing over the next three-to-five years.

We acknowledge that markets can remain speculative and would hesitate to predict exactly when the trend favoring lower-quality stocks will reverse. But we would also point out that as we head toward the back half of 2025, there is still a great deal of uncertainty about tariffs and trade policy, a war in Europe and tensions in the Middle East. Throw in the uncertainty about interest rate policy and there are plenty of factors that could shake the equity markets and sour investor sentiment. We’ve tilted the Fund’s portfolio a little more defensively in light of these risks, targeting a market beta of 0.50 for the portfolio, down from 0.55 last quarter.

Amid the uncertainties, we’re hewing closely to the investment philosophy and process that has always guided the Wasatch investment team. Within our long portfolio, we remain focused on high-quality companies that have a proven track record of earnings growth, a history of strong free cash flow generation, and that are led by management teams we know and trust. That high-quality focus was admittedly a headwind to the Fund’s performance relative to the Russell 2500 Index in the second quarter. But in times of macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, these are precisely the types of businesses we want to own.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Mick Rasmussen

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance data, visit wasatchglobal.com. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. The Advisor may absorb certain expenses, without which total returns would have been lower. Wasatch Funds will deduct a 2% redemption fee on Fund shares held 60 days or less. Equity investing involves risks, including potential loss of the principal amount invested. Short selling incurs significant unique risks, including potentially unlimited downside risk, high short-sale related expenses, and unavailability of securities to sell short, among others, all of which could negatively impact the performance of the Fund. Additionally, the Fund may not be able to borrow the securities it intends to sell short.

Investment in the Fund will involve market risks associated with different types of investment decisions than those made for a typical “long only” fund. The Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund is not in any way connected to or sponsored, endorsed, sold or promoted by the London Stock Exchange Group plc and its group undertakings (collectively, the “LSE Group”). These indexes are calculated by or on behalf of FTSE International Limited or its affiliate, agent or partner. The LSE Group does not accept any liability whatsoever to any person arising out of (a) the use of, reliance on or any error in these indexes or (b) investment in or operation of the Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund or the suitability of these indexes for the purpose to which they are being put by Wasatch Global Investors.

Beta is a quantitative measure of the volatility of a given security relative to the overall market. A beta above one is more volatile than the overall market, while a beta below one is less volatile.

The “cloud” is the internet. Cloud-computing is a model for delivering information-technology services in which resources are retrieved from the internet through web-based tools and applications, rather than from a direct connection to a server.

Earnings growth is a measure of growth in a company’s net income over a specific period, often one year.

Operating margin, also called operating profit margin, is the amount a company makes on every dollar of sales after deducting variable costs but before accounting for interest or taxes. Operating margin is calculated by dividing the company’s operating income by its net sales.

Return on assets (ROA) measures a company’s profitability by showing how many dollars of earnings a company derives from each dollar of assets it controls.

“Risk-on” is when investors are seeking the potentially higher returns of riskier assets and put money back in the market willing to risk the money, thus risk on.

Valuation is the process of determining the current worth of an asset or company.

data.title: Eli Lilly Q2 Earnings: Ignore Oral Weight Loss Bombshell, This Is A Buy The Dip Moment
data.description:
Investment Overview

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), the world's largest pharmaceutical globally by market cap, announced its Q2 earning earlier today, exceeding the Street's expectations on revenues of $15.6bn, and on adjusted earnings per share of $6.31, and hiking full-year revenue guidance from $58bn - $61bn, to $60bn - $62bn.

Unfortunately for the Indianapolis-headquartered Pharma, earnings outperformance was overshadowed by the release of worse than expected data from >3k patient study of its oral weight loss pill, Orforglipron, which could "only" achieve an average of 12.4% mean weight loss (27.3 lbs), compared to 0.9% in the placebo arm. Analysts had apparently hoped to see reductions of >15%.

It's pretty clear which bit of news the market has homed in on, as Lilly stock has crashed by >15% in trading so far today, to ~$630 per share, which gives the company a market cap valuation of just over $600bn, it's lowest value in over 18 months.

Meanwhile, shares of Novo Nordisk (NVO), Lilly's great rival in the glucagon-like peptide-1 ("GLP-1") space, have gained >5% in trading so far today - Novo hopes to secure approval for an oral version of its Wegovy (semaglutide) weight loss drug before the end of this year having already had its formal submission accepted by the FDA.

Before we consider the orforglipron data in more detail, let's firstly review Lilly's - overall impressive - Q2 results.

Eli Lilly Q2 Results Overview - 35% Sequential Growth For Tirzepatide A "Wow" Moment

Lilly's Q2 revenue figure of $15.6bn was up an impressive 38% year-on-year, somewhat justifying the massive hype around its tirzepatide drug, which is in fact a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetic - Novo's semaglutide targets GLP-1 only.

Lilly says that its "incretin analogs" (incretin is a hormone involved stimulating insulin release and reducing glucagon secretion) share of the market has increased to 57% of total prescriptions i.e. tirzepatide - which is approved to treat type 2 diabetes under the brand name Mounjaro, and to treat obesity under the brand name Zepbound - is outselling semaglutide - approved in the same two indications as Ozempic / Wegovy.

In Q2, Mounjaro and Zepbound earned respectively $5.2bn and $3.4bn revenues, up 68% and 172$ year-on-year respectively, and up 35% and 46% sequentially. The two meds together accounted for ~55% of Eli Lilly's revenues, underlining the importance of tirzepatide to the company, its future, and its share price and valuation.

Altogether, Lilly's cardiometabolic division earned $11.34bn of revenues, up 51% year-on-year, with T2D med Trulicity earning $1.1bn of revenues, down 12% year-on-year, and Jardiance (heart failure) and Humalog (human insulin) earning $503m and $690m respectively, down 20% and 10% respectively.

Breast cancer med Verzenio earned $1.5bn revenues, up 12% year-on-year, helping Lilly's oncology division earn $2.4bn revenues, or 16% of total revenues, while Taltz sales of $848m, and Olumiant sales of $246m, up 3% and 8%, helped the immunology division generate $1.3bn revenues. The neuroscience division earned just $344m of revenues, with Alzheimer's Disease drug Kisunla, once believed to have double-digit billion peak revenue potential, earned $49m, up from $22m in Q1.

Lilly racked up total operating expenses of $6.24bn, earning operating income of $6.9m (margin of 44%), while net income came to $5.7bn (margin of 37%), and earnings per shares ("EPS") came to $6.29. Full 2024 guidance was shared as per the below slide, taken from today's earnings presentation:

As we can see, EPS has been hiked, as well as revenues, meaning the current forward price to sales and price to earnings ratios for Lilly, based on current share price and valuation, are ~9.9x, and 29x, which is high for the "Big Pharma" sector. Sector averages by my estimation are typically <5x, and <20x.

Eli Lilly's current dividend pays $1.5 per quarter, which translates to an annual yield of ~0.8%.

Orforglipron Bombshell Adds To Growing Fears Around Lilly's Obesity Prospects

Until today, it had been widely accepted that Lilly's portfolio of weight loss oriented GLP drugs was streets ahead of the competition - even Novo Nordisk.

Novo's semaglutide lost out to tirzepatide in a head-to-head study (albeit one conducted by Lilly), and its next generation drug Cagrisema - a combination of cagrilintide, a dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist, and semaglutide - flopped in a Phase 3 study, achieving mean 15.7% weight loss at 68 weeks, when Novo had talked up its chances of achieving >25%.

Orforglipron data shared in April from a Phase 3 study showed that it reduced A1C by an average of 1.3% to 1.6% across doses, and a secondary endpoint showed that it "reduced weight by an average 16.0 lbs (7.9%) at the highest dose in a key secondary endpoint" at 40 weeks.

The data set up an approval shot, and the chances are, orforglipron will still be approved, most likely next year, but the latest data suggests it may not be nearly as appealing to patients as was once expected.

While oral administration may be preferable to many, those using injectable Zepbound / Mounjaro, can theoretically lose >20% of their body fat, compared to 12.4% using orforglipron over the same timeframe, while oral Wegovy may be the outstanding option as patients in clinical studies achieved more or less the same results as those using injectable Wegovy - >15%.

To make matters even worse for Lilly, potentially, a recent study of higher dose semaglutide (7.2mg) showed that patients lost a mean 20.7% weight at 68 weeks - matching torzeaptide's pivotal study results.

Another threat to Lilly's weight loss business comes from a different source - well, two different sources in fact. Firstly, CVS Health (CVS) the health insurance and pharmacy benefit manager ("PBM"), has dropped tirzepatide from its preferred formulary lists, and opted to prioritize coverage of semaglutide instead.

Secondly, companies such as Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), the online pharmacy, continue to sell "compounded" versions of both semaglutide and tirzepatide, i.e. non FDA approved versions of the drug, exploiting a loophole in policy that allows it to provide a compounded version if a patient has a "personalized" dose requirement.

On its earnings call today, Lilly's CEO David Ricks told analysts that law enforcement and the FDA need to "step up their game" in order to put an end to this process, owing to the risk posed to patients by non-FDA-approved versions of GLP drugs; however, an alternative solution might be for Lilly to drop its price for Zepbound / Mounjaro, potentially making compounders unprofitable, given they cannot compete in terms of economies of scale.

Lilly is already selling tirzepatide at low prices via its DTC channels, and that is a strategy that may become increasingly important in the face of President Trump's war on drug pricing, as it cuts out the much disliked PBM - another one of Trump's targets.

Drug Pricing Pressures / Tariffs Also Give Eli Lilly Headache

Lilly's management could be forgiven for reaching out for some of its migraine therapy, Emgality - $201m sales in Q2 - when contemplating the Trump administration's threat to both introduce a "most favored nation" ("MFN") drug pricing policy - i.e. Americans pay the same amount for their drugs as whichever nation globally charges the least - and slap tariffs of up to 250% on pharmaceutical imports.

Lilly has announced plans to take its investment in domestic manufacturing to the $50bn mark since 2020, with four new manufacturing sites planned, creating >3k new jobs in the process, but the latest demand from the government to implement MFN within 60 days - a demand that almost certainly cannot be met without Pharma valuations coming crashing down, if at all - puts enormous pressure on the sector, and may spell the beginning of the end of double-digit profit margins, let alone the 37% margin reported by Lilly today. With that said, Trump tried and failed to implement an MFN policy in his first term.

Looking Ahead - Should Investors Be Looking To Buy, Sell, or Hold LLY Stock After Today's News?

If we take a look at Lilly's current clinical pipeline of new drug prospects, we can see that it is somewhat low on new molecular entities, and arguably, somewhat high on weight loss / diabetes:

Imlunestrant - a selective estrogen receptor degrader ("SERD") indicated for breast cancer, is an intriguing prospect with "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) potential, and remternetug a potential improvement on Kisunla, which has a difficult safety profile to sell to patients. Jayprica, in heme cancers, has multi-billion-dollar potential, but otherwise, it is mostly within the twin indications of weight loss / T2D where Lilly will be judged.

In fairness, bulls may look at the >$8.5bn tirzepatide earned in a single quarter, and the >$60bn Lilly hopes to generate in revenues in 2025, which would place it behind only Pfizer (PFE) among global Pharmaceutical companies, and wonder what all the fuss is about? Analysts at Goldman's recently downgraded their expectations for the global obesity market, but still believe that it will be worth >$95bn by 2030, and Lilly currently controls >50% of that market.

Tirzepatide will very likely soon be the world's best-selling drug - I'd estimate it may generate >$33bn revenues this year - and has a strong chance of securing further approvals in e.g. cardiovascular and kidney disease, and longer-term perhaps even in Alzheimer's and even oncology, analysts have speculated - it is a unique and extremely valuable drug.

It's also worth mentioning Lilly's recent acquisition of Verve Therapeutics, working on an in-vivo cholesterol lowering therapy that, if successful, would almost certainly become a double-digit billion selling asset. And finally, worth mentioning is retatrutide, Lilly's pipeline GLP-1 that targets GIP, GLP, and glucagon, earning it the nickname "Triple G" (tirzepatide is sometimes referred to as "twincretin" due to its dual mechanism of action). Early retatrutide data has been compelling, and more is due before the end of year, the company has promised.

In my last note on Lilly, published in June, I suggested that "if you don't buy on weight loss promise now, you probably never will" and wrote:

In order for Lilly to fulfill my prior prediction and achieve a market cap of >$1 trillion, you truly have to believe that GLP-1 drugs are like nothing we have ever seen before, and capable of drumming up >$150bn per annum in revenues for Lilly, I'd speculate—a forward price to sales ratio of ~6.7x (based on a $1 trillion valuation).

You also must believe that Lilly can manage the price of its drug while still making a high profit margin, >40%, I'd speculate, and maintaining a price-to-earnings ratio of <15x, even as its valuation creeps towards $1 trillion.

Tirzepatide earned $6.2bn of revenues in Q1 2025, and that figure would need to surpass $20bn per quarter, combined with Lilly's other GLP-1 drugs, in order to support a long-term bull thesis for Lilly stock, in my view.

Some of these figures may seem ridiculous today, especially after a comparatively poor showing for orforglipron, but then again, it's said that one in three Americans - >100m people - are obese and would benefit from treatment with a GLP-1 drug. As I wrote in a recent note on Novo Nordisk:

therefore, if Novo charges $1,000 per annum - one tenth of what it charges today—and enjoys a 50% market share, it will earn $50bn per annum - in the U.S. alone.

The same can be said of Eli Lilly - once you begin doing the math, a revenue target of ~$150bn per annum begins to seem more and more reasonable.

Investing in Lilly today is a highly subjective matter, and my trillion dollar market cap target seems an awfully long way off, however $20bn of revenue per quarter could be achieved almost by this time next year, based on current growth rates, and if this is not a bull thesis to get excited about, I am not sure what is.

Today's sell off is therefore, in my view, the chance that I did not believe would come around when I wrote my last "Hold" recommendation, i.e. to pick up Lilly stock at <$650 per share.

This could be a good time to "be greedy when others are fearful", although I'll acknowledge the strategy is a risky one, given current headwinds.

data.title: Cloudflare: Policing The Internet
data.description:
As every month passes, Cloudflare, Inc. (NYSE:NET) expands the massive AI cloud opportunity. The company now promotes the ability to protect original content from AI players, further expanding the market opportunity, but the stock has grown far beyond the boost to sales. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock due to the vast disconnect in the valuation.

Multiple Expansion

After another strong quarter, the name of the game for Cloudflare is multiple expansion. The connectivity cloud company reported the following Q2'25 results:

Our prior research highlighted how Cloudflare was set to produce $2.09 billion in 2025 sales with a market cap of $61 billion. The stock now has an $80 billion market cap with a 2025 sales target of $2.115 billion.

In essence, Cloudflare has guided up the revenue targets for the year by just $25 million and the market cap has soared $19 billion in the 6-month period. Hopefully, most people can fully grasp the disconnect with reality here.

In the course of the last year the forward P/S multiple has surged from a low of 16x to over 34x now. Cloudflare entered the year at $100 and now the stock is over $200 without much changing in the financial landscape for the business.

As with many companies tied to AI and cloud computing, the business can spin strong prospects, but Cloudflare isn't actually producing the bigger upside due to AI. If anything, AI is just helping the business maintain strong growth just below 30%.

The company definitely has some exciting business prospects due to the concept of policing the internet now. Cloudflare is leading the fight for organic content creators to prevent AI crawlers from scraping content without paying to feed AI summaries.

Neil Vogel, the CEO of Dotdash Meredith, whose brands include People and Southern Living, made the following statement about using Cloudflare to protect their IP and ensure the licensing deal with OpenAI obtains content while preventing unauthorized access.

People who create intellectual property need to be protected or no one will make intellectual property anymore.

Cloudflare launched the product on July 1 requiring AI crawlers to obtain explicit permission from a website before scraping data. The cloud connectivity company announced a whole host of publishers in support of their technology, but the WSJ report suggests the success of the product is unknown as AI crawlers always seem to find ways around roadblocks.

The company offers the Cloudflare Radar with AI Insights highlighting 77% of crawl traffic is related to AI training now, not search. The data suggests the company could have a strong growth driver for the years ahead, if Cloudflare is successful at becoming a gatekeeper for forcing AI firms to pay for original content.

Vast Disconnect

As highlighted above, the stock remains vastly disconnected. Cloudflare trades at over 35x 2025 sales targets. The stock doesn't dip to below 10x sales until 2031 when sales are forecast to reach $7.7 billion.

In essence, investors could expect 6+ years when the stock trades flat to down. Cloudflare could rally higher from here in the short term, but traditionally a stock with 20% growth targets doesn't trade much above 10x sales targets.

The cloud connectivity company is highly profitable, but analysts just forecast a $1.11 EPS in 2026. Cloudflare trades at nearly 200x EPS targets for next year.

While Cloudflare is free cash flow positive, again the valuation is too hard to reconcile when no level of cash flows can justify the stock value at 35x sales. The company produced $33 million in FCF in Q1 and $86 million in the 1H'25, but it's a fraction of the market cap at $80 billion.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Cloudflare has an impressive business with consistent growth just below 30%. Investors need to comprehend how the company has to grow at the forecasted rates for 6+ years to just warrant the current stock price.

The chart shows a stock breaking out and likely riding the current momentum higher, but an investor clearly has to exit the position when the momentum ends.

data.title: Grab: Fierce GMV Growth In All Segments
data.description:
With the stock market now mirroring the jitters in the macroeconomy, I continue to emphasize very careful stock-picking as the best way to beat the markets for the remainder of FY25. On top of rotating out of large-cap growth stocks, I'm also favoring shifting more of my portfolio out of the U.S. and increasing my exposure to relatively undervalued international plays.

Grab (NASDAQ:GRAB), in my view, is one of the best international diversification plays available that also offers "growth at a reasonable price." Already well on the way to becoming the "super-app" of Southeast Asia, investors have long been skeptical on this stock: indicating that opportunistic investors can still buy into this name at a relative discount. Grab is about ~flat since the start of January, and a recent ~10% dip in the stock after reporting a strong earnings quarter in late July offers a good "buy the dip" opportunity.

I last wrote a bullish opinion on Grab in May, when the stock was trading near $5 per share. Since Grab shares have dipped slightly since then, I'm taking advantage of the recent downside in the stock to increase my exposure to the stock, especially amid a recent acceleration in the company's pace of Deliveries GMV growth. I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion here.

In my view, these are the top reasons to be bullish on Grab:

  • Multiple business lines to address a massive growing economy in Southeast Asia. Grab is well on its way to becoming the Uber (UBER) of Southeast Asia, with a wide-ranging array of businesses ranging from mobility, deliveries, and financial services. The company operates in one of the fastest-growing demographics in the world with a rising middle class.
  • Product innovation. Grab is the king of optionality: the company notes that increasing new options like Saver Deliveries and Priority Deliveries have broadened its appeal to a larger set of customers, fueling strong GMV expansion.
  • Advertising opportunity. The company is boosting its revenue take rates (as a percentage of GMV) by pushing additional advertising revenue streams, which is still relatively small at a ~$240 million annualized run rate. Ad penetration as a percentage of deliveries GMV is still low, indicating plenty of opportunity to expand into more merchants.
  • Substantial liquidity to drive growth initiatives. The company had $3.9 billion of cash liquidity on its balance sheet as of the end of Q2, giving the company plenty of financial flexibility to pursue its growth initiatives.

From a valuation perspective: Grab certainly isn't cheap, but it offers a reasonable price when we consider the pace of its earnings growth. At current share prices near $4.80, Grab trades at a market cap of $19.44 billion. After we net off the $3.88 billion of cash against $1.91 billion of debt on the company's most recent balance sheet, Grab's resulting enterprise value is $17.47 billion.

For FY25, Grab has maintained its full-year outlook at $460-$480 million in adjusted EBITDA, representing 50% y/y growth at the midpoint, on 19-22% y/y growth in revenue.

This positions Grab's valuation at 37x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. While I acknowledge that it would be a stretch to call Grab a value stock by any means, I also think it's far more difficult to use near-term multiples for a high-growth company that's growing adjusted EBITDA at a ~50% pace as a true barometer of value.

For example, as shown above, Uber trades at a cheaper ~22x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple - but at the same time, Uber's ~35% pace of EBITDA growth in its most recent quarter pales in comparison to Grab's 70% growth in Q2. On top of an accelerating growth pace in the Deliveries segment that is helping to generate much better adjusted EBITDA margins, I'm willing to give Grab a pass on its near-term valuation multiples.

This does not mean the stock is without risk - in particular, investors should be cognizant of the following two potential risks the company faces on top of valuation:

  • Higher incentives spending. Grab is nudging up its spending on incentives (discounts to consumers as well as earnings incentives paid to drivers) to ~10% of GMV, up from ~9% last year. This indicates a highly competitive environment, especially from homegrown rivals like GoJek.
  • Growing loan portfolio may present credit risks in a shaky global macroeconomy. Trade deals have just been signed in some of Grab's most critical markets: Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines. Though the tariff structure provides clarity, the higher tariff baseline may have an adverse impact on local economies, which may threaten the company's growing loan book.

This being said, I see far more upside than downside in Grab, especially after its post-earnings share price movement offers a compelling buy-the-dip opportunity. Stay long here.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Grab's latest quarterly results in greater detail, beginning with the company's mobility division, which represents roughly one-third of overall revenue and GMV.

Mobility GMV grew 19% y/y in the quarter, or 16% on a constant-currency basis (as a stronger U.S. dollar on a y/y basis against many key currencies, especially the Indonesian Rupiah, has boosted Grab's dollar-denominated results). Growth accelerated two points sequentially versus Q1, but primarily due to currency impacts. Importantly, in the mobility segment, Grab was able to moderate incentives spending to 7.8% of GMV, down 30bps y/y versus 8.1% in the year-ago quarter, which helped the company boost mobility segment adjusted EBITDA to 8.7%, 50bps higher y/y.

It's in the Deliveries segment, however, that Grab is showcasing the most promising results. As shown in the chart below, GMV for Deliveries grew 22% y/y in Q2, accelerating six points versus 16% growth in Q1 - and we note as well that Deliveries segment growth had hovered in the much weaker single digit range for much of FY23.

Here, the company is pointing to several drivers for accelerating growth. Its push for affordability, particularly in releasing Saver deliveries (which offer delivery fees that are substantially lower than on-demand delivery, or even sometimes free delivery) has increased Grab's appeal in a tough economy.

On top of this: in Indonesia, one of new president Prabowo Subianto's signature policies is offering free nutritious meals to all school-aged children (concerns over the fiscal impact of this policy is one of the key reasons the Indonesian Rupiah has slid against the dollar over the past year, which has also been a boost to Grab's as-reported results). Grab has been partnering with the Indonesian government to deliver a chunk of these meals, per CEO Anthony Tan's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

For example, in Indonesia, we participated in the pilot phase of Makan Bergizi Gratis, which is the free nutritious meal program, very important for the Indonesian government. As part of this initiative, we delivered nutritious meals to over 1,500 students across 7 elementary schools across many parts in Indonesia, and we collaborated with 9 local MSME merchants in these areas to provide healthier meals, but also fostered greater brand loyalty among our student customer segment.

In financial services, Grab's loan portfolio surged 78% y/y to $708 million, while adjusted EBITDA losses remained roughly flat y/y.

From a total company perspective, Grab's adjusted EBITDA soared 70% y/y to $109 million, representing a 13.3% margin on revenue, improving 370bps y/y from 9.7% in the year-ago Q2.

Again, it's the fact of Grab's rapid pace of EBITDA expansion, on top of its numerous top-line initiatives to drive revenue growth in both the mobility and delivery segments, that keep me bullish on Grab despite a rich valuation.

Key takeaways

Grab remains one of my top international diversification plays. The company is continuing to demonstrate solid market share in fast-growing Southeast Asia, while also bolstering its adjusted EBITDA margins despite incentives spending. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: I Have Been Waiting For A Drop Like This
data.description:
Shares of Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) crashed more than 18% on Wednesday in the regular trading session after the AI server maker reported fourth fiscal quarter results the night before that missed estimates. The company also issued a weak outlook for Q1 '26 and suffered margin pressure in its core server business.

I believe the 18% price drop, as was the case with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), is unjustified and just another occurrence of exaggerated volatility after an earnings report for the server company. With a forward price-to-earnings ratio of less than 14X, Super Micro Computer’s valuation includes a very high safety margin and the company has a favorable risk profile on the drop as well. I recommend investors buy the drop and see the correction as another engagement opportunity to build a long position in a leading AI hardware company at a discounted valuation factor.

Previous rating

I recommended Super Micro Computer as a Strong Buy after the company got back into compliance with Nasdaq 100 listing rules earlier this year, following an extended period of uncertainty surrounding the company’s outstanding financial reports: A Contrarian AI play. In the fourth quarter, Super Micro Computer saw a deceleration of its top-line growth, but the server company nonetheless generated solid profitability. While margin issues amid higher operating costs are an issue, Super Micro Computer is nonetheless set to grow and poised for massive top-line growth in the next fiscal year.

SMCI misses EPS and revenue estimates

The server company reported adjusted earnings of $0.41 per share on Tuesday after the market closed, which fell $0.03 per share short of Wall Street’s consensus estimate. Super Micro Computer reported revenue of $5.8B, which missed the average prediction by $156M.

Super Micro Computer generated $5.76B in revenue, showing 8% year-over-year growth. Compared to the previous quarter, the AI hardware company saw an 11 PP growth deceleration, on a Y/Y basis. On a Q/Q basis, Super Micro Computer still benefited from very decent growth, +25%, amid strong demand for the company's server and storage systems. Like other AI hardware companies, Super Micro Computer fundamentally benefits from increasing CapEx spending in the AI-enabled Data Center infrastructure market and the company has relationships with leading companies in the market providing and acquiring high-performing GPU solutions such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), or xAI.

What added selling pressure on Super Micro Computer's shares on Wednesday was that the AI server maker experienced continual pressure on its non-GAAP gross margins in its core business… which is a key metric for companies in the hardware market. In the fourth quarter, Super Micro Computer generated a non-GAAP gross margin of 9.6%, showing a contraction of 0.7 PP which is a relatively small percentage amount and should not have caused such an aggressive selloff on Wednesday, in my opinion.

Despite Super Micro Computer suffering a sequential drop-off in its gross margins, due to higher operating expenses, the company generated a profit of $195.2M. The earnings figure also saw a decline amid cost pressures, including higher stock-based compensation ("SBC") expenses, but Super Micro Computer was nonetheless profitable.

A key factor in the decline of Super Micro Computer's gross margins was higher non-GAAP operating expenses, including both higher SBC and R&D operating costs, offset by lower general and administrative expenses. Total non-GAAP operating expenses increased to $238.9M, showing a 29% Y/Y increase. Compared to Super Micro Computer's revenues, expenses also grew slightly faster, by 4 PP, explaining the dip in margins year-over-year.

50% revenue growth next year implies strong participation potential in AI market expansion

The outlook for FY 2025 calls for $33.0B in revenue, implying a massive 50% year-over-year top-line growth rate… which highlights that the company is still expected to see material expansion which is directly linked to Super Micro Computer's strong position in the server infrastructure market. However, SMCI's guidance for fiscal Q1 '26, especially with regard to earnings, disappointed: the server company said it expects to earn $0.40-0.52/share (non-GAAP) in the current quarter while analysts were looking for $0.59/share. This miss added to concerns about margins and led to a stock selloff that I believe investors should take advantage of.

SMCI's valuation

Because of Wednesday's 18% crash, shares of Super Michael Computer are now trading at a material discount to the pre-report price level. Now, shares of Super Micro Computer are trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 13.4X, which is about 3% below the 1-year average P/E ratio of 13.8X.

However, other hardware-driven AI plays in the Data Center market, like Nvidia, AMD, or Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) are trading at significantly higher price-to-earnings ratios, in large part because companies are rapidly ramping up their spending on AI-enabled Data Center infrastructure. While Nvidia and AMD are more chip-design plays, they benefit from growing AI adoption in the Data Center market. Nvidia, AMD and Broadcom are trading at forward P/E ratios of 27.8X (in AMD's case) or higher, with Broadcom now being the most expensive AI hardware play in the industry group (with a P/E ratio of 36.6X).

In my opinion, Super Micro Computer could easily re-price to a ~20X P/E ratio if it stabilizes its non-GAAP margins and focuses on growing its top-line in the core server market (which would be the best-case outcome for SMCI). Super Micro Computer also remained widely profitable in the fourth quarter, indicating that the market overreacted to the server maker's earnings report. A 20X P/E ratio, applied to the consensus EPS estimate for next year, implies a fair value of at least $53/share. However, if the server market keeps expanding due to accelerating AI spend, the company has considerable EPS growth potential and earnings estimates could reset higher as well.

Risks with SMCI

The biggest risk for a Super Micro Computer, as I see it, relates to the company experiencing continual margin pressure in its core server business which could incrementally create negative sentiment overhang for the server maker’s shares… if SMCI fails to turn this trend around. What would potentially change my mind on Super Micro Computer is if the company were not to be able to participate in the broader expansion of the Data Center market or if it were to see continual pressure on its margin profile.

Final thoughts

Super Micro Computer missed on the top and the bottom line for its fourth fiscal quarter and experienced sequential margin pressure with the non-GAAP gross margin sliding below 10%. At the same time, fundamentals in the Data Center industry seem to be intact and should ultimately benefit server companies like SMCI with a focus on AI-optimized hardware.

Super Micro Computer has been extremely volatile over the last year, so I do not consider the 18% post-earnings drop to be especially unsettling. In my opinion, the drop on Wednesday represents a new engagement opportunity for investors with a long-term investment horizon who want to participate in the overall growth of AI-driven server infrastructure.
